title: Cellular & Molecular Biology
subtitle: How genetic information is stored, copied, expressed, and engineered — the molecular machinery underneath every living cell
methodology: METHODOLOGY · Every entry anchors to a landmark study, paper, clinical case or theoretical text. The hint explains the idea in plain language; the note names the primary publication and flags replication status, effect-size revisions and known controversies. Studies and effects carry a replicability badge — 🟢 robust · 🟡 nuanced or reduced · 🔴 failed or severely contested. Map pins mark the institution where the work was carried out.

categories.label: Theories & Models
categories.subtitle: The frameworks that organise how genetic information is stored, copied, and expressed

entries.label: The Central Dogma of Molecular Biology
entries.hint: Genetic information flows in one direction: DNA is copied into RNA, and RNA is translated into protein — not the reverse. Francis Crick proposed this as the organising logic of molecular biology, carefully noting it forbade information flowing from protein back into nucleic acid, not that RNA could never be copied back into DNA (a distinction later vindicated by the discovery of reverse transcriptase).
entries.note: Crick, F.H.C. (1958). 'On Protein Synthesis.' Symposia of the Society for Experimental Biology, 12, 138–163. Retroviruses like HIV genuinely do reverse the DNA-to-RNA step (see ef9), which is often mistaken for disproving the dogma — it doesn't, since Crick's original statement never forbade RNA-to-DNA copying, only protein-to-nucleic-acid.

entries.label: The Operon Model
entries.hint: Bacteria don't waste energy making enzymes they don't need — a cluster of genes for digesting a specific sugar sits under the control of a single on/off switch, flipped by a repressor protein that falls off the DNA only when that sugar is actually present. François Jacob and Jacques Monod's operon model, built on their work with the E. coli lac operon, was the first mechanistic explanation of how gene expression is regulated at all.
entries.note: Jacob, F., Monod, J. (1961). 'Genetic regulatory mechanisms in the synthesis of proteins.' Journal of Molecular Biology, 3(3), 318–356. Shared the 1965 Nobel Prize with André Lwoff. Bacterial operons remain the clearest textbook example of gene regulation; eukaryotic regulation turned out to be substantially more complex (see co4, th-level chromatin regulation), involving mechanisms operons alone don't capture.

entries.label: Chemiosmotic Theory
entries.hint: Cells make their energy currency, ATP, using a mechanism that looks nothing like ordinary chemistry: pumping protons across a membrane to build up a concentration gradient, then letting them flow back through a molecular turbine (ATP synthase) that uses that flow to bolt a phosphate onto ADP. Peter Mitchell proposed this indirect, gradient-driven mechanism when most biochemists still assumed energy transfer required a direct chemical intermediate — and was widely dismissed for over a decade before being vindicated.
entries.note: Mitchell, P. (1961). 'Coupling of Phosphorylation to Electron and Hydrogen Transfer by a Chemi-Osmotic type of Mechanism.' Nature, 191(4784), 144–148. Awarded the 1978 Nobel Prize in Chemistry, largely alone, after the field's initial deep skepticism gave way to overwhelming experimental confirmation. Mitchell developed and confirmed the theory largely at his own privately funded research institute after leaving conventional academia.

entries.label: The Signal Hypothesis
entries.hint: A newly made protein doesn't need an outside address label to reach the right place in the cell — the address is written into the protein itself, as a short stretch of amino acids at one end that gets recognised and physically threaded into the endoplasmic reticulum if the protein is destined for secretion or a membrane. Günter Blobel's signal hypothesis explained how a cell sorts thousands of different proteins to dozens of different destinations without external help.
entries.note: Blobel, G., Dobberstein, B. (1975). 'Transfer of proteins across membranes.' Journal of Cell Biology, 67(3), 835–851. Blobel received the 1999 Nobel Prize, unusually, entirely alone. The core mechanism has been extensively confirmed and extended to protein sorting throughout the cell, well beyond the original secretory-pathway case.

entries.label: Endosymbiotic Theory
entries.hint: Mitochondria and chloroplasts were once free-living bacteria, engulfed by an ancestral cell in a partnership that became permanent rather than predatory — which is why both organelles still carry their own small, circular genome and divide independently of the rest of the cell, echoes of their former life as separate organisms. Lynn Margulis revived and rigorously argued this old, largely abandoned idea with genuinely compelling molecular evidence.
entries.note: Margulis (as L. Sagan), L. (1967). 'On the origin of mitosing cells.' Journal of Theoretical Biology, 14(3), 225–274 — rejected by roughly fifteen journals before publication. Now overwhelmingly confirmed by mitochondrial and chloroplast DNA sequencing, which shows clear bacterial ancestry; considered one of the more dramatic vindications of a once-marginal idea in twentieth-century biology.

entries.label: The Wobble Hypothesis
entries.hint: There are 61 possible codons for amino acids but noticeably fewer than 61 distinct tRNA molecules to read them — the genetic code shouldn't work with the machinery cells actually have. Francis Crick proposed that pairing at the third codon position is looser ("wobbles") than standard base pairing, letting a single tRNA recognise more than one codon, which resolved the apparent shortfall.
entries.note: Crick, F.H.C. (1966). 'Codon—anticodon pairing: The wobble hypothesis.' Journal of Molecular Biology, 19(2), 548–555. Confirmed by subsequent structural and biochemical work on tRNA-mRNA pairing; the wobble position is also why the genetic code is described as "degenerate" — most amino acids are specified by more than one codon, largely differing only at this third position.

entries.label: The Prion Hypothesis
entries.hint: An infectious agent doesn't need any genetic material at all — a misfolded protein alone can be infectious, by physically contacting normal copies of the same protein and forcing them to adopt its own misfolded shape, which then goes on to misfold the next one, and the next. Stanley Prusiner proposed this to explain scrapie and Creutzfeldt-Jakob disease, directly contradicting the field's near-universal assumption that all infectious agents require DNA or RNA.
entries.note: Prusiner, S.B. (1982). 'Novel proteinaceous infectious particles cause scrapie.' Science, 216(4542), 136–144. Met with intense skepticism and open ridicule for years — Prusiner was awarded the 1997 Nobel Prize once purified prion protein alone was shown sufficient to transmit disease, one of the most dramatic reversals of scientific consensus in modern biology (see db7 for the contested history in more depth).

entries.label: The RNA World Hypothesis
entries.hint: Modern cells split genetic storage (DNA) and catalytic work (mostly proteins) into two different kinds of molecule — but early life couldn't have started that way, since DNA needs proteins to replicate and proteins need DNA to be specified, a chicken-and-egg problem. The RNA world hypothesis proposes that RNA alone did both jobs at first, storing information and catalysing its own replication, before DNA and proteins later took over each role separately.
entries.note: Gilbert, W. (1986). 'The RNA world.' Nature, 319(6055), 618. The hypothesis gained real experimental support once ribozymes — RNA molecules with genuine catalytic activity — were discovered by Cech and Altman in the early 1980s (Nobel 1989); it remains a leading but not universally accepted origin-of-life framework, since no self-replicating RNA system has been demonstrated from scratch under plausible early-Earth conditions.

entries.label: Cell Cycle Checkpoint Theory
entries.hint: The cell cycle isn't just a fixed-duration timer running to completion regardless of circumstances — dedicated surveillance mechanisms actively check that each stage genuinely finished correctly (DNA fully replicated, no damage present) before allowing the next stage to begin, and can halt the whole cycle if something is wrong. Leland Hartwell's checkpoint concept reframed the cell cycle as a monitored, error-correcting process rather than a simple clock.
entries.note: Hartwell, L.H., Weinert, T.A. (1989). 'Checkpoints: controls that ensure the order of cell cycle events.' Science, 246(4930), 629–634. Hartwell shared the 2001 Nobel Prize with Tim Hunt and Paul Nurse, whose cyclin and CDK discoveries identified the molecular machinery the checkpoints actually control. Checkpoint failure — cells dividing despite damaged DNA — is now understood as a central, near-universal feature of cancer.

entries.label: Chargaff's Rules
entries.hint: In DNA from any organism, the amount of adenine always equals the amount of thymine, and the amount of guanine always equals the amount of cytosine — a strange, consistent numerical regularity Erwin Chargaff measured with no explanation for why it should be true. It turned out to be the single most important clue Watson and Crick used to work out that DNA's bases pair up A-with-T and G-with-C across the double helix.
entries.note: Chargaff, E. (1950). 'Chemical specificity of nucleic acids and mechanism of their enzymatic degradation.' Experientia, 6(6), 201–209. Chargaff was reportedly unimpressed when he later met Watson and Crick, and felt his own contribution was under-credited relative to theirs — a minor but genuine footnote in the DNA-structure story's larger, better-known credit disputes (see db1).

categories.label: Landmark Studies
categories.subtitle: The defining experiments — and exactly what they found

entries.label: Watson and Crick's DNA Structure
entries.hint: Combining Rosalind Franklin's X-ray diffraction images of DNA with Chargaff's base-pairing ratios, James Watson and Francis Crick built a physical model showing DNA is a double helix of two strands running in opposite directions, held together by complementary base pairing. The structure itself instantly suggested the copying mechanism: each strand could serve as a template for building its complementary partner.
entries.note: Watson, J.D., Crick, F.H.C. (1953). 'Molecular structure of nucleic acids: A structure for deoxyribose nucleic acid.' Nature, 171(4356), 737–738. One of the most consequential papers in the history of science; also one of the most contested on questions of credit — Franklin's unpublished X-ray data was shown to Watson without her knowledge or permission, a matter of ongoing historical and ethical debate (see db1, fg2).

entries.label: The Hershey-Chase Experiment
entries.hint: Using radioactive labels — sulphur for viral protein, phosphorus for viral DNA — Alfred Hershey and Martha Chase tracked which part of a virus actually entered a bacterium during infection and directed the production of new viruses. Only the DNA went in; the protein coat stayed outside. It settled a genuine, live scientific argument over whether genes were made of protein or DNA, still unresolved eight years after Avery's more indirect evidence.
entries.note: Hershey, A.D., Chase, M. (1952). 'Independent functions of viral protein and nucleic acid in growth of bacteriophage.' Journal of General Physiology, 36(1), 39–56 — widely nicknamed the "Blender Experiment" for the kitchen blender used to shear viruses off the bacterial surface. Hershey shared the 1969 Nobel Prize; the finding was decisive in convincing the wider field that DNA, not protein, is the hereditary material.

entries.label: The Meselson-Stahl Experiment
entries.hint: Matthew Meselson and Franklin Stahl grew bacteria in a heavy nitrogen isotope, then switched them to normal nitrogen and tracked DNA density across generations using centrifugation. The pattern they found — each new DNA molecule containing exactly one old strand and one newly made strand — confirmed semi-conservative replication, exactly as the Watson-Crick structure predicted, and ruled out two competing models.
entries.note: Meselson, M., Stahl, F.W. (1958). 'The replication of DNA in Escherichia coli.' Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences, 44(7), 671–682 — often called "the most beautiful experiment in biology" for how cleanly its result distinguished between three competing hypotheses in a single, simple design. Robustly confirmed and never seriously challenged since.

entries.label: The Avery-MacLeod-McCarty Experiment
entries.hint: Oswald Avery's team purified DNA alone from a disease-causing strain of bacteria and showed it could transform a harmless strain into the disease-causing form, passing the trait on to future generations of bacteria — direct evidence that DNA, not protein, carries hereditary information. Many leading scientists at the time remained unconvinced, assuming DNA was too chemically simple to encode something as complex as heredity; full acceptance took another eight years, until Hershey and Chase's experiment.
entries.note: Avery, O.T., MacLeod, C.M., McCarty, M. (1944). 'Studies on the chemical nature of the substance inducing transformation of pneumococcal types.' Journal of Experimental Medicine, 79(2), 137–158 — building on Frederick Griffith's 1928 observation that some "transforming principle" could pass traits between bacterial strains, without yet identifying what it was. Avery is now widely regarded as having deserved, but never received, a Nobel Prize for this work.

entries.label: Cracking the First Codon
entries.hint: Marshall Nirenberg and Heinrich Matthaei built a synthetic RNA strand made of nothing but repeating uracil, added it to a cell-free protein-making mixture, and got a protein made of nothing but the amino acid phenylalanine. That single result deciphered the first codon (UUU means phenylalanine) and opened the floodgates: the entire genetic code was fully worked out within about five years using variations of the same technique.
entries.note: Nirenberg, M.W., Matthaei, J.H. (1961). 'The dependence of cell-free protein synthesis in E. coli upon naturally occurring or synthetic polyribonucleotides.' Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences, 47(10), 1588–1602. Nirenberg shared the 1968 Nobel Prize; the codon table produced by this line of work is now one of the most robustly confirmed universal features of life, with only minor variations found in a small number of organisms and organelles.

entries.label: Kornberg's Discovery of DNA Polymerase
entries.hint: Arthur Kornberg isolated the actual enzyme that builds new DNA strands from a template — the first direct look at replication's molecular machinery rather than just its outcome — and used it to synthesise DNA outside a living cell for the first time. The achievement made replication a chemistry problem that could be studied enzyme by enzyme, not just an abstract process inferred from genetics.
entries.note: Kornberg, A., Lehman, I.R., Bessman, M.J., Simms, E.S. (1956). 'Enzymic synthesis of deoxyribonucleic acid.' Biochimica et Biophysica Acta, 21(1), 197–198. Kornberg shared the 1959 Nobel Prize. The specific enzyme he isolated (DNA polymerase I) later turned out to play a more minor role in normal bacterial replication than his initial work suggested — several further polymerases were discovered later — but the enzymatic approach it pioneered is foundational to all subsequent replication research.

entries.label: The First Recombinant DNA
entries.hint: Using restriction enzymes as molecular scissors, Stanley Cohen and Herbert Boyer cut DNA from two different bacterial plasmids and spliced the pieces together into a single, new molecule that a living bacterium could replicate and pass to its offspring — the first time DNA had been deliberately engineered by combining genetic material from different sources into one functional whole.
entries.note: Cohen, S.N., Chang, A.C.Y., Boyer, H.W., Helling, R.B. (1973). 'Construction of Biologically Functional Bacterial Plasmids In Vitro.' Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences, 70(11), 3240–3244. Directly founded the biotechnology industry (Boyer co-founded Genentech in 1976); Stanford's patent on the technique became one of the most lucrative university patents in history, and the ease of the method immediately raised the biosafety questions that led to the 1975 Asilomar Conference on recombinant DNA guidelines.

entries.label: The Invention of PCR
entries.hint: Kary Mullis conceived the core idea of repeatedly heating and cooling a DNA sample with short primer sequences and a copying enzyme, so that each cycle doubles the amount of a specific target sequence — turning a trace amount of DNA into billions of copies within hours. The original protocol required manually adding fresh enzyme after every heating cycle, since it was destroyed by the heat each time; a heat-stable enzyme (see pathology-immunology::me3) made the process practical to automate three years later.
entries.note: Saiki, R.K., Scharf, S., Faloona, F., Mullis, K.B., Horn, G.T., Erlich, H.A., Arnheim, N. (1985). 'Enzymatic Amplification of β-Globin Genomic Sequences and Restriction Site Analysis for Diagnosis of Sickle Cell Anemia.' Science, 230(4732), 1350–1354. Mullis shared the 1993 Nobel Prize in Chemistry for the underlying idea. He became a genuinely controversial figure in later life, publicly promoting AIDS denialism and other claims well outside scientific consensus — unrelated to, and not undermining, the technical achievement of PCR itself.

entries.label: The Discovery of RNA Interference
entries.hint: Injecting double-stranded RNA into a roundworm silenced genes with a matching sequence far more powerfully than injecting single-stranded RNA of the same sequence — an oddly specific and unexpected result that revealed a previously unknown, evolutionarily ancient mechanism cells use to silence their own genes and defend against viruses. The discovery moved unusually fast from basic finding to Nobel Prize, just eight years later.
entries.note: Fire, A., Xu, S., Montgomery, M.K., Kostas, S.A., Driver, S.E., Mello, C.C. (1998). 'Potent and specific genetic interference by double-stranded RNA in Caenorhabditis elegans.' Nature, 391(6669), 806–811. Shared the 2006 Nobel Prize. RNA interference rapidly became both a standard laboratory tool for silencing specific genes and the basis of a new drug class (RNAi therapeutics), the first of which was approved in 2018.

entries.label: CRISPR-Cas9 as a Programmable Editing Tool
entries.hint: Bacteria use CRISPR-Cas9 as an immune system, storing snippets of viral DNA to recognise and cut up the same virus if it attacks again. Jennifer Doudna and Emmanuelle Charpentier showed the cutting mechanism could be reprogrammed with a simple synthetic guide RNA to cut almost any chosen DNA sequence — turning a narrow bacterial immune defence into a general-purpose, cheap, and precise gene-editing tool almost overnight.
entries.note: Jinek, M., Chylinski, K., Fonfara, I., Hauer, M., Doudna, J.A., Charpentier, E. (2012). 'A Programmable Dual-RNA-Guided DNA Endonuclease in Adaptive Bacterial Immunity.' Science, 337(6096), 816–821. Shared the 2020 Nobel Prize in Chemistry — the first Nobel science prize awarded to a team of two women. The speed and low cost of CRISPR editing compared to earlier gene-editing methods (zinc-finger nucleases, TALENs) drove its extremely rapid adoption across research and, more controversially, clinical and germline applications (see db3).

entries.label: The Discovery of Telomerase
entries.hint: Every time a chromosome is copied, the very ends are left slightly shorter than before — a side effect of how DNA polymerase works that would eventually erode important genes if left unchecked. Carol Greider, working as a graduate student in Elizabeth Blackburn's lab, discovered an enzyme that rebuilds these protective chromosome-end caps (telomeres), explaining how some cells — stem cells, germ cells, most cancer cells — can keep dividing indefinitely while ordinary cells eventually stop.
entries.note: Greider, C.W., Blackburn, E.H. (1985). 'Identification of a specific telomere terminal transferase activity in Tetrahymena extracts.' Cell, 43(2), 405–413, building on earlier genetic work with Jack Szostak (1982). Blackburn, Greider, and Szostak shared the 2009 Nobel Prize. Telomerase is normally switched off in most adult human cells, and its reactivation is now recognised as a near-universal feature of cancer cells achieving unlimited replication (see pathology-immunology::th5).

categories.label: Effects & Phenomena
categories.subtitle: Named, replicable patterns in gene expression, cell behaviour, and molecular inheritance

entries.label: Alternative Splicing
entries.hint: A single gene can produce several different proteins, depending on which stretches of its RNA transcript (exons) get stitched together and which get cut out before translation. The discovery that genes are broken into pieces at all, let alone that the pieces could be reassembled in different combinations, was a genuine surprise — it's a major reason roughly 20,000 human genes can produce a much larger number of distinct proteins.
entries.note: Chow, L.T., Gelinas, R.E., Broker, T.R., Roberts, R.J. (1977). 'An amazing sequence arrangement at the 5' ends of adenovirus 2 messenger RNA.' Cell, 12(1), 1–8, published alongside an independent, simultaneous paper by Berget, Moore and Sharp. Sharp and Roberts shared the 1993 Nobel Prize. Robustly confirmed as pervasive — most human multi-exon genes are now known to be alternatively spliced.

entries.label: X-Inactivation (Lyonization)
entries.hint: Female mammals carry two X chromosomes, but each cell randomly and permanently silences one of them early in development — so every female mammal is a genetic mosaic, a patchwork of cells expressing one X or the other. The visible proof is hiding in plain sight: a calico cat's blotchy coat pattern is the direct, visible signature of which X got silenced in which patch of skin cells.
entries.note: Lyon, M.F. (1961). 'Gene action in the X-chromosome of the mouse (Mus musculus L.).' Nature, 190(4773), 372–373. Robustly confirmed and now understood at the molecular level (mediated by the long non-coding RNA Xist); the process is random in most tissue but not perfectly so, and skewed X-inactivation is clinically relevant in some X-linked disease carriers.

entries.label: Genomic Imprinting
entries.hint: Some genes behave completely differently depending on whether they were inherited from the mother or the father, even though the DNA sequence is identical either way — a direct violation of the simple assumption that a gene's effect depends only on its sequence. Mouse embryos engineered to have two maternal or two paternal genome copies (instead of the normal one of each) fail to develop properly, showing both parental contributions are genuinely, separately necessary.
entries.note: McGrath, J., Solter, D. (1984). 'Completion of mouse embryogenesis requires both the maternal and paternal genomes.' Cell, 37(1), 179–183. Mediated by epigenetic marks (DNA methylation) laid down differently in eggs and sperm; imprinting disorders in humans (Prader-Willi and Angelman syndromes) result from losing the correctly imprinted copy of the same small region of chromosome 15.

entries.label: Replicative Senescence (the Hayflick Limit)
entries.hint: Normal human cells grown in culture don't divide forever — after roughly 40 to 60 divisions they permanently stop, entering a state distinct from death called senescence. Leonard Hayflick's finding directly contradicted the prevailing assumption of the time, based on flawed earlier cell-culture work, that normal cells could proliferate indefinitely if simply given the right conditions.
entries.note: Hayflick, L., Moorhead, P.S. (1961). 'The serial cultivation of human diploid cell strains.' Experimental Cell Research, 25(3), 585–621. Now understood to be driven substantially by telomere shortening (see st11) — each division trims a little off the chromosome ends, and senescence sets in once telomeres become critically short, a limit that cancer cells characteristically escape by reactivating telomerase.

entries.label: MicroRNA-Mediated Gene Regulation
entries.hint: Cells manufacture their own small RNA molecules — microRNAs — whose entire job is to bind matching sequences on other RNA transcripts and dial their expression up or down, a naturally occurring layer of fine-tuning distinct from the artificially triggered RNA interference Fire and Mello later characterised. The first one found, lin-4, initially looked like a strange quirk of worm development; thousands of microRNAs have since been identified across essentially all animals and plants.
entries.note: Lee, R.C., Feinbaum, R.L., Ambros, V. (1993). 'The C. elegans heterochronic gene lin-4 encodes small RNAs with antisense complementarity to lin-14.' Cell, 75(5), 843–854. Largely overlooked for years until RNA interference (see st9) made the broader importance of small regulatory RNAs suddenly obvious in retrospect; microRNA dysregulation is now implicated in numerous cancers and is an active area of therapeutic development.

entries.label: Horizontal Gene Transfer
entries.hint: Bacteria don't only inherit genes from a parent cell — they can pick up DNA directly from their environment, from a virus, or from a neighbouring cell of an entirely different species, and put it to immediate use. It's the same basic phenomenon Frederick Griffith stumbled onto in 1928 when a harmless bacterial strain absorbed a "transforming principle" from a dead virulent one, decades before anyone knew that principle was DNA — and it's the single biggest reason antibiotic resistance genes spread between bacterial species so quickly.
entries.note: Ochman, H., Lawrence, J.G., Groisman, E.A. (2000). 'Lateral gene transfer and the nature of bacterial innovation.' Nature, 405(6784), 299–304. Robustly documented as a major, ongoing force in bacterial genome evolution — comparative genomics estimates a substantial fraction of many bacterial genomes arrived via horizontal transfer rather than vertical inheritance (see pathology-immunology::ef10 for its clinical consequence in antibiotic resistance).

entries.label: Reverse Transcription
entries.hint: Certain viruses run the central dogma's usual direction in reverse, copying their RNA genome back into DNA once inside a host cell, using an enzyme — reverse transcriptase — that biology wasn't supposed to have. David Baltimore and Howard Temin discovered the enzyme independently and simultaneously, published side by side in the same issue of Nature, resolving a genuine dispute over whether such retroviruses even existed as RNA-based agents at all.
entries.note: Baltimore, D. (1970). 'RNA-dependent DNA Polymerase in Virions of RNA Tumour Viruses.' Nature, 226(5252), 1209–1211; Temin, H.M., Mizutani, S. (1970). 'RNA-dependent DNA Polymerase in Virions of Rous Sarcoma Virus.' Nature, 226(5252), 1211–1213. Shared the 1975 Nobel Prize with Renato Dulbecco. This is not a violation of the central dogma as Crick originally, carefully stated it (see th1) — HIV's use of exactly this enzyme is what reverse-transcriptase inhibitor drugs target (see pathology-immunology::st8).

entries.label: Chromatin Remodelling and Histone Modification
entries.hint: DNA doesn't float freely in the nucleus — it's wound tightly around spool-like histone proteins, and whether a gene gets expressed often depends less on the DNA sequence itself than on how loosely or tightly that particular stretch of chromatin is packaged. Chemically tagging the histones (acetylation, methylation) loosens or tightens the packaging, switching genes on or off without changing a single DNA letter — the molecular basis of much of epigenetics.
entries.note: Allfrey, V.G., Faulkner, R., Mirsky, A.E. (1964). 'Acetylation and Methylation of Histones and Their Possible Role in the Regulation of RNA Synthesis.' Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences, 51(5), 786–794 — an early, largely correct proposal that took over two decades to be fully mechanistically confirmed once specific histone-modifying enzymes were identified in the 1990s.

entries.label: Receptor Tyrosine Kinase Signalling
entries.hint: When a growth factor binds a receptor tyrosine kinase on the cell surface, two receptor molecules pair up and phosphorylate each other, creating docking sites that recruit a whole cascade of downstream signalling proteins — turning one external binding event into a coordinated internal instruction to grow, divide, or survive. It's the mechanism behind most growth-factor signalling, and mutations that lock these receptors permanently "on" are a recurring driver of cancer.
entries.note: Schlessinger, J. (2000). 'Cell Signaling by Receptor Tyrosine Kinases.' Cell, 103(2), 211–225. Robustly established and directly clinically actionable — several targeted cancer drugs (e.g. trastuzumab, see pathology-immunology::ap4) work by blocking an overactive receptor tyrosine kinase.

entries.label: Chaperone-Assisted Protein Folding
entries.hint: A newly made protein chain doesn't reliably fold itself into its correct working shape on its own, especially under cellular stress — dedicated helper proteins called molecular chaperones physically shepherd it through folding, preventing the tangled, sticky misfolded states that would otherwise clump together and damage the cell. Heat-shock proteins, a major chaperone family, are named for how strongly cells ramp up their production the moment temperature stress threatens to unfold everything at once.
entries.note: Ellis, R.J. (1987). 'Proteins as Molecular Chaperones.' Nature, 328(6129), 378–379 — the paper that coined "molecular chaperone" in its modern sense. Chaperone failure and the resulting protein aggregation is now implicated in a range of diseases, from cystic fibrosis (a misfolded chloride channel) to neurodegenerative conditions involving protein aggregates.

categories.label: Key Concepts
categories.subtitle: The building blocks and vocabulary every student must command

entries.label: Codons and the Genetic Code
entries.hint: Genetic information is written in three-letter words: each codon, a specific sequence of three DNA or RNA bases, specifies one amino acid to add to a growing protein chain (or a signal to stop). With 64 possible codons but only 20 amino acids, most amino acids are specified by more than one codon — a redundancy (see th6) that turns out to buffer many mutations against actually changing the resulting protein.
entries.note: The complete codon table was worked out by the mid-1960s through combined work by Marshall Nirenberg (see st5), Har Gobind Khorana, and Robert Holley, who shared the 1968 Nobel Prize. The code is close to universal across all known life, with only a small number of documented exceptions in certain organelles and organisms.

entries.label: Chromatin Structure: Histones and Nucleosomes
entries.hint: DNA in a human cell would stretch about two metres if laid end to end, yet it fits inside a nucleus a few micrometres across — solved by wrapping the DNA around spool-like histone protein cores, forming repeating bead-on-a-string units called nucleosomes, which then coil further into denser chromatin. This packaging isn't just for storage: how tightly a given stretch is coiled directly controls whether the genes in it can be read (see ef8).
entries.note: Kornberg, R.D. (1974). 'Chromatin Structure: A Repeating Unit of Histones and DNA.' Science, 184(4139), 868–871 — Roger Kornberg, son of Arthur Kornberg (see st6), working independently on an entirely different problem, later won his own Nobel Prize in 2006 for unrelated work on RNA polymerase and transcription.

entries.label: The Cell Cycle Phases
entries.hint: A dividing cell moves through a fixed sequence of stages — growth (G1), DNA replication (S), further preparation (G2), and division itself (M) — each driven forward by a specific protein, cyclin, whose levels rise and fall exactly in step with the stage it controls, then gets destroyed to allow the next stage to begin. Discovering that cyclins existed at all, and that cells destroy them on a schedule, explained how the cycle keeps its own time.
entries.note: Evans, T., Rosenthal, E.T., Youngblom, J., Distel, D., Hunt, T. (1983). 'Cyclin: A protein specified by maternal mRNA in sea urchin eggs that is destroyed at each cleavage division.' Cell, 33(2), 389–396. Tim Hunt shared the 2001 Nobel Prize with Hartwell and Nurse (see th9); cyclins pair with partner enzymes, cyclin-dependent kinases (CDKs), to actually execute each transition.

entries.label: G-Protein Coupled Receptors
entries.hint: The single largest family of cell-surface receptors all share the same basic architecture: a protein that snakes back and forth across the cell membrane seven times, changing shape when a signal molecule binds outside and activating an internal partner protein inside — from receptors for adrenaline and light-sensing rhodopsin in the eye to the receptors targeted by roughly a third of all prescription drugs.
entries.note: Dixon, R.A.F., et al., Lefkowitz, R.J. (1986). 'Cloning of the gene and cDNA for mammalian beta-adrenergic receptor and homology with rhodopsin.' Nature, 321(6065), 75–79 — the paper that first showed a hormone receptor and a light receptor share the same seven-pass structural family. Lefkowitz shared the 2012 Nobel Prize with Brian Kobilka.

entries.label: Reversible Phosphorylation as a Molecular Switch
entries.hint: Attaching or removing a single phosphate group can flip a protein between an active and inactive state almost instantly — a fast, reversible, cheap molecular switch that cells use constantly throughout signalling, metabolism, and the cell cycle. Edmond Fischer and Edwin Krebs discovered the principle studying a single metabolic enzyme; it turned out to generalise to a large fraction of all cellular regulation.
entries.note: Fischer, E.H., Krebs, E.G. (1955). 'Conversion of phosphorylase b to phosphorylase a in muscle extracts.' Journal of Biological Chemistry, 216(1), 121–132. Shared the 1992 Nobel Prize. Roughly a third of all human proteins are now estimated to be regulated by phosphorylation at some point, added and removed by the large kinase and phosphatase enzyme families.

entries.label: Michaelis-Menten Enzyme Kinetics
entries.hint: An enzyme speeds up a reaction only up to a point — as substrate concentration keeps rising, the reaction rate levels off at a maximum, because the enzyme molecules are all already working as fast as they can. Leonor Michaelis and Maud Menten's simple mathematical model of this saturation curve, and the two constants that describe it, remains the standard first framework for describing how any enzyme behaves.
entries.note: Michaelis, L., Menten, M.L. (1913). 'Die Kinetik der Invertinwirkung.' Biochemische Zeitschrift, 49, 333–369. Maud Menten, a Canadian physician-scientist, had to leave North America to pursue this research at a time when few institutions there permitted women to lead independent research; the model remains foundational and is taught essentially unchanged over a century later.

entries.label: Protein Domains and Structural Motifs
entries.hint: Large proteins are often built from smaller, independently folding modules — domains — that can recur across many otherwise unrelated proteins, each domain contributing a specific function like binding DNA or catalysing a reaction, mixed and matched like interchangeable parts across the genome. Recognising this modularity became possible only once a protein's full three-dimensional structure could actually be seen.
entries.note: Kendrew, J.C., Bodo, G., Dintzis, H.M., Parrish, R.G., Wyckoff, H., Phillips, D.C. (1958). 'A Three-Dimensional Model of the Myoglobin Molecule Obtained by X-Ray Analysis.' Nature, 181(4610), 662–666 — the first protein structure ever solved. Kendrew shared the 1962 Nobel Prize with Max Perutz, whose parallel work solved haemoglobin's structure.

entries.label: Stem Cell Potency
entries.hint: Not all stem cells are equally flexible: totipotent cells can become any cell type including placental tissue, pluripotent cells can become any cell type in the body but not placental tissue, and multipotent cells are restricted to a limited family of related cell types (blood stem cells making only blood and immune cells, for instance). James Till and Ernest McCulloch's bone marrow experiments gave stem cell biology its first rigorous, quantitative footing.
entries.note: Till, J.E., McCulloch, E.A. (1961). 'A Direct Measurement of the Radiation Sensitivity of Normal Mouse Bone Marrow Cells.' Radiation Research, 14(2), 213–222 (see anatomy-histology::st8 for the same landmark from the haematopoiesis angle). The potency hierarchy is now central to both developmental biology and regenerative medicine, including induced pluripotent stem cell technology.
